behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily an algorithm-quality boundary inside deterministic route-planning logic. You can directly feed coordinate sets, compute heuristic length, compute optimal length with an exact solver in-test, and assert ratio <= 1.5. No I/O or external system is required to reproduce real failures in this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Call the heuristic function with many deterministic and randomized coordinate sets (sizes 2..20). For each set, compute the true optimal tour length using an exact TSP solver implementation used only in tests, then assert heuristic_length / optimal_length <= 1.5.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: Real failures are likely at module seams: distance output + road-type speed config lookup + estimator composition. A single-function unit test can miss miswiring (wrong road-type key, wrong unit conversion path between modules). No external infra is needed, but cross-module wiring is.
behaviors.test_description: Run the estimator through the real in-process pipeline: route legs with known distances and road types, real config loader/object, and time-window calculator. Assert per-leg and total ETAs match expected values (including unit conversions and rounding rules).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic arithmetic and threshold logic at one object/function boundary. Real failures (off-by-one, sign/units mistakes) are reproducible with direct inputs and outputs; no I/O needed.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the validator with route time and remaining shift-hour values around the boundary (equal, just under, just over, far over). Assert rejection status and exact excess duration returned.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is orchestration across geocoding client response handling, skip logic, and optimizer invocation. Pure unit tests of each piece can miss that the pipeline still includes failed stops or fails to report skips. External real HTTP is not strictly required for this behavior if the client contract is exercised with realistic response objects.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the service pipeline with a fake/in-memory geocoding adapter that returns valid coordinates for most addresses and an explicit no-results response for one address. Assert the final route excludes that stop, optimization runs on remaining stops, and skipped-address reporting contains exactly the failed address.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: The key risk is not just dedup logic, but that deduped data is correctly propagated into route optimization and visit generation. A unit test of dedup alone can miss re-expansion/regression later in the pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run the in-process pipeline with repeated identical addresses carrying different delivery items. Assert output contains one stop for that address, items are merged correctly, and the final route visits that location once.
